expanded_description: The Distance Formula calculates the length of a line segment in the coordinate plane using the coordinates of its endpoints. Derived from the Pythagorean Theorem, this formula is one of the most powerful tools in coordinate geometry, enabling precise distance calculations without physical measurement. It connects algebra and geometry by expressing the ancient theorem in coordinate language.

core_concept.principle: The Distance Formula states that the distance d between two points (x₁, y₁) and (x₂, y₂) is $d = \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2}$. This formula is the Pythagorean Theorem expressed in coordinates: the horizontal and vertical differences form the legs of a right triangle, and the segment itself is the hypotenuse.
core_concept.explanation: Think of the segment between two points as the hypotenuse of a right triangle. The horizontal leg is the difference in x-coordinates. The vertical leg is the difference in y-coordinates. The Distance Formula uses the Pythagorean Theorem: square both legs, add them, and take the square root to find the hypotenuse—which is exactly the length of the segment. Instead of measuring with a ruler, you calculate with arithmetic.

image.alt: A coordinate plane showing segment AB from A(x₁, y₁) to B(x₂, y₂). The segment forms the hypotenuse of a right triangle. The horizontal leg is labeled |x₂ − x₁|, the vertical leg |y₂ − y₁|. The Distance Formula is displayed, with arrows connecting the legs to the squared terms under the radical.
image.caption: The Distance Formula: the Pythagorean Theorem in coordinates. The segment is the hypotenuse of a right triangle formed by horizontal and vertical changes.

sections.section_title: From the Pythagorean Theorem to the Distance Formula
sections.introduction: The Distance Formula is the Pythagorean Theorem written in coordinate notation. Every segment in the coordinate plane can be viewed as the hypotenuse of a right triangle whose legs are parallel to the axes. Understanding this connection transforms a memorized formula into a logical geometric relationship.

introduction_image.alt: Two panels: (1) A right triangle with legs a and b, hypotenuse c, and the Pythagorean Theorem a² + b² = c². (2) The same triangle on a coordinate grid, with legs now labeled |x₂−x₁| and |y₂−y₁|, and the hypotenuse labeled d. The Distance Formula d = √((x₂−x₁)² + (y₂−y₁)²) is shown as the translation of a² + b² = c² into coordinate language.
introduction_image.caption: Left: Pythagorean Theorem with side lengths. Right: The same triangle in coordinates, producing the Distance Formula.

key_concept.definition: The Distance Formula $d = \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2}$ calculates the length of the segment connecting two points in the coordinate plane. It is derived by constructing a right triangle with the segment as hypotenuse and the horizontal and vertical differences as legs, then applying the Pythagorean Theorem $a^2 + b^2 = c^2$.
key_concept.context: The Distance Formula is one of the two foundational coordinate geometry formulas (along with the Midpoint Formula). It is used to find side lengths of polygons in the coordinate plane, to determine if triangles are scalene, isosceles, or equilateral, to calculate perimeters and areas, and to write equations of circles.

image.alt: Step-by-step derivation: (1) Plot A(x₁, y₁) and B(x₂, y₂). (2) Draw right triangle with third point C(x₂, y₁). (3) Label legs: AC = |x₂−x₁|, BC = |y₂−y₁|. (4) Apply Pythagorean Theorem: (AC)² + (BC)² = (AB)². (5) Substitute and take square root: AB = √((x₂−x₁)² + (y₂−y₁)²).
image.caption: Deriving the Distance Formula: build the right triangle, apply the Pythagorean Theorem.

process_flow.process: Plot the Two Points and Identify Coordinates
process_flow.description: Identify the (x, y) coordinates of both endpoints. It does not matter which point is (x₁, y₁) and which is (x₂, y₂) because the differences are squared, eliminating any sign issues.
process_flow.mechanism: Write the coordinates clearly. The order of subtraction does not matter: (x₂ − x₁)² = (x₁ − x₂)². The same is true for y. This symmetry means you can always subtract in whichever order gives positive numbers for easier mental math.
process_flow.result: Two ordered pairs ready for substitution into the formula.

image.alt: Coordinate plane with A(1, 3) and B(7, 11) plotted. Coordinates labeled. A right triangle drawn with the horizontal leg from (1,3) to (7,3) and vertical leg from (7,3) to (7,11). The segment AB is the hypotenuse.
image.caption: Step 1: Plot the points, identify coordinates, and visualize the right triangle.

process_flow.process: Calculate the Horizontal and Vertical Differences
process_flow.description: Find the change in x (Δx = x₂ − x₁) and the change in y (Δy = y₂ − y₁). These are the lengths of the legs of the right triangle.
process_flow.mechanism: Subtract the x-coordinates to get the horizontal leg length. Subtract the y-coordinates to get the vertical leg length. These differences represent how far apart the points are horizontally and vertically. Square both differences.
process_flow.result: The squared lengths of the two legs: (Δx)² and (Δy)².
applications: These squares represent the areas of squares built on the legs.

image.alt: The right triangle with legs labeled Δx = 6 and Δy = 8. Squares are drawn on each leg: a 6×6 square on the horizontal leg (area 36) and an 8×8 square on the vertical leg (area 64). The squares visually represent (Δx)² and (Δy)².
image.caption: Step 2: Find horizontal change (6) and vertical change (8). Square them: 36 and 64.

process_flow.process: Apply the Pythagorean Theorem and Take the Square Root
process_flow.description: Add the squares of the legs, then take the square root to find the hypotenuse—the distance between the points.
process_flow.mechanism: $d = \sqrt{(\Delta x)^2 + (\Delta y)^2}$. This is the Pythagorean Theorem: the square of the hypotenuse equals the sum of the squares of the legs. Taking the square root gives the distance. Distance is always positive (or zero if the points coincide).
process_flow.result: The exact or approximate length of the segment.
applications: This is a 6-8-10 right triangle (a 3-4-5 triangle scaled by 2).

image.alt: The complete right triangle with the square on the hypotenuse shown (area 100). The equation d² = 36 + 64 = 100 is displayed, with d = √100 = 10. The 6-8-10 triangle is labeled as a 3-4-5 Pythagorean triple.
image.caption: Step 3: d² = 36 + 64 = 100, so d = 10. A 6-8-10 triangle (Pythagorean triple).

classifications.value: When points share the same y-coordinate or x-coordinate, the Distance Formula simplifies to the absolute difference of the changing coordinate.
characteristics: Horizontal segment (y₁ = y₂): $d = |x_2 - x_1|$ — no Pythagorean Theorem needed
Vertical segment (x₁ = x₂): $d = |y_2 - y_1|$ — simply the vertical distance
The full formula still works but one term becomes zero under the radical
These are the one-dimensional cases of the Distance Formula
classifications.behavior: For horizontal segments, the right triangle collapses to a single horizontal line. For vertical segments, it collapses to a vertical line. The formula handles both correctly: $\sqrt{(\Delta x)^2 + 0} = |\Delta x|$.

examples.process: Distance between points on the same horizontal line
examples.explanation:
A(3, 5) and B(11, 5). Same y, so horizontal segment.

$d = \sqrt{(11-3)^2 + (5-5)^2} = \sqrt{8^2 + 0} = \sqrt{64} = 8$.

Simply: |11 − 3| = 8 units apart.

image.alt: Horizontal segment from (3,5) to (11,5). Distance labeled 8. The 'right triangle' is flat — height is 0. Formula shown simplifying to d = √(8² + 0) = 8.
image.caption: Horizontal segment: y's are equal, so distance is just |x₂ − x₁|.

classifications.type: Pythagorean Triples in the Distance Formula
classifications.value: When the coordinate differences form a Pythagorean triple (like 3-4-5, 6-8-10, 5-12-13), the distance is an integer—no square root simplification needed.
characteristics: If Δx and Δy match the legs of a triple, the distance is the hypotenuse value
Multiples of triples also work: (6,8) → 10, (9,12) → 15, (12,16) → 20
Recognizing triples saves calculation time and confirms answers
classifications.behavior: When the coordinate differences are 3 and 4 (or multiples), the distance is 5 (or the multiple of 5). This is a quick mental check—if you get a messy decimal for a triple, you made an arithmetic error.

examples.explanation:
A(2, 3) and B(14, 8). Δx = 12, Δy = 5.

12 and 5 are the legs of a 5-12-13 Pythagorean triple.

$d = \sqrt{12^2 + 5^2} = \sqrt{144 + 25} = \sqrt{169} = 13$.

The distance is exactly 13.

image.alt: Segment from (2,3) to (14,8). Right triangle with legs 12 and 5. The 5-12-13 triple is highlighted. Distance = 13. Label: '5-12-13 Pythagorean Triple — distance is an integer.'
image.caption: 5-12-13 triple: legs 5 and 12 give hypotenuse 13. Quick and exact.

classifications.value: When (Δx)² + (Δy)² is not a perfect square, the distance remains in simplified radical form.
characteristics: Leave the answer as a simplified radical unless a decimal approximation is requested
Simplify: $\sqrt{20} = 2\sqrt{5}$, $\sqrt{72} = 6\sqrt{2}$, $\sqrt{50} = 5\sqrt{2}$
Exact radical form is preferred in geometry—it is the exact value
Decimal approximations are used for practical measurements
classifications.behavior: Most random pairs of points will not produce a perfect square under the radical. Simplifying the radical gives the exact mathematical answer. Only approximate if the problem asks for a decimal.

examples.explanation:
A(1, 2) and B(5, 6). Δx = 4, Δy = 4.

$d = \sqrt{4^2 + 4^2} = \sqrt{16 + 16} = \sqrt{32}$.

Simplify: $\sqrt{32} = \sqrt{16 \times 2} = 4\sqrt{2}$.

Exact distance: $4\sqrt{2}$ units (approximately 5.66 units).

image.alt: Segment from (1,2) to (5,6). Right triangle with legs 4 and 4 (an isosceles right triangle). Distance = √32 = 4√2. The simplification steps shown: √32 = √(16×2) = √16 × √2 = 4√2.
image.caption: Exact answer: 4√2. Simplify radicals; don't just give decimals.

image.alt: Comprehensive diagram: segment AB on coordinate plane forming right triangle. Horizontal leg, vertical leg, and hypotenuse labeled. Three example types shown: horizontal segment (simple), Pythagorean triple (integer distance), and radical distance (simplified). The Distance Formula displayed prominently.
image.caption: Distance Formula overview: right triangle construction, formula, and three common outcome types.

sections.section_title: Applying the Distance Formula to Polygons
sections.introduction: The Distance Formula determines side lengths of polygons plotted in the coordinate plane. This allows classification of triangles (scalene, isosceles, equilateral) and quadrilaterals (square, rectangle, rhombus) based on calculated side lengths, transforming geometric classification into algebraic calculation.

introduction_image.alt: A coordinate plane showing three polygons: a triangle with vertices labeled, a rectangle, and a rhombus. For each, side lengths are calculated using the Distance Formula. Arrows point from vertices to distance calculations.
introduction_image.caption: The Distance Formula calculates side lengths of polygons, enabling classification by side length.

key_concept.definition: By applying the Distance Formula to each pair of consecutive vertices of a polygon, you can determine all side lengths. These lengths reveal the polygon's classification: equilateral (all sides equal), isosceles (two sides equal), scalene (no sides equal), or specific quadrilaterals based on side relationships.
key_concept.context: Coordinate proofs use the Distance Formula to prove geometric properties algebraically. Instead of relying on diagrams or measurements, you can prove a triangle is isosceles by showing two side lengths are equal using exact coordinate calculations.

image.alt: Triangle with vertices A(1,2), B(7,2), C(4,8). Three distance calculations shown: AB = 6, BC = √(3²+6²) = √45, AC = √(3²+6²) = √45. Since AC = BC, the triangle is isosceles. Two equal sides are highlighted.
image.caption: Triangle ABC: AC = BC = √45, so it is isosceles. Proven by the Distance Formula.

classifications.value: Calculate all three side lengths using the Distance Formula. Compare them to classify the triangle.
characteristics: Equilateral: all three sides equal (d₁ = d₂ = d₃)
Isosceles: exactly two sides equal (d₁ = d₂ ≠ d₃, or any permutation)
Scalene: no sides equal (all three distances different)
Only side lengths are needed for this classification—angles are not required
classifications.behavior: The Distance Formula provides objective, exact side lengths. You can prove a triangle is isosceles by showing two Distance Formula calculations yield the same result.

image.alt: Triangle with vertices A(0,0), B(8,0), C(4,6). Sides AC and BC highlighted in matching colors and labeled √52. Side AB labeled 8. Label: 'AC = BC → Isosceles triangle (proven by Distance Formula).'
image.caption: AC = BC = √52. The Distance Formula proves the triangle is isosceles.

classifications.value: Use the Distance Formula on all four sides (and sometimes diagonals) to verify if a quadrilateral is a specific type.
characteristics: Square: all four sides equal AND diagonals equal
Rectangle: opposite sides equal AND diagonals equal
Rhombus: all four sides equal (diagonals may differ)
Parallelogram: opposite sides equal
classifications.behavior: The Distance Formula replaces 'it looks like a square' with mathematical proof. Calculate the four side lengths and the two diagonals, then check the defining properties of the quadrilateral.

examples.process: Verifying a quadrilateral is a rhombus
examples.explanation:
Vertices: P(1, 1), Q(5, 2), R(6, 6), S(2, 5).

PQ = $\sqrt{4^2 + 1^2} = \sqrt{17}$

QR = $\sqrt{1^2 + 4^2} = \sqrt{17}$

RS = $\sqrt{4^2 + 1^2} = \sqrt{17}$

SP = $\sqrt{1^2 + 4^2} = \sqrt{17}$

All four sides equal → rhombus (need to check diagonals for square).

image.alt: Quadrilateral PQRS with vertices labeled. All four sides marked with matching tick marks and labeled √17. Calculations shown for each side. Diagonals PR and QS also calculated (different lengths) confirming rhombus, not square.
image.caption: All four sides = √17. The Distance Formula proves it is a rhombus.

worked_examples.title: Distance Formula and Polygon Classification
worked_examples.problem:
1) Classify triangle ABC with vertices A(2, 3), B(10, 3), C(6, 9).

2) Determine if quadrilateral WXYZ with W(0, 0), X(5, 0), Y(8, 4), Z(3, 4) is a parallelogram.

3) Show that triangle DEF with D(1, 1), E(5, 1), F(3, 5) is isosceles.

step_by_step.explanation:
A(2,3) and C(6,9):

$AC = \sqrt{(6-2)^2 + (9-3)^2} = \sqrt{4^2 + 6^2} = \sqrt{16+36} = \sqrt{52} = 2\sqrt{13}$

BC = AC = $2\sqrt{13}$, AB = 8. Two sides equal → isosceles triangle.

WX = $\sqrt{(5-0)^2 + 0^2} = 5$

YZ = $\sqrt{(8-3)^2 + (4-4)^2} = 5$ → WX = YZ ✓

XY = $\sqrt{(8-5)^2 + (4-0)^2} = \sqrt{9+16} = 5$

WZ = $\sqrt{(3-0)^2 + (4-0)^2} = \sqrt{9+16} = 5$ → XY = WZ ✓

Both pairs of opposite sides equal → parallelogram.

image.alt: Three diagrams: (1) Isosceles triangle ABC with AB=8, BC=AC=2√13. (2) Parallelogram WXYZ with opposite sides marked equal (5 and 5). (3) Isosceles triangle DEF with DE=4, EF=DF=2√5.
image.caption: Classifying polygons: isosceles triangles and parallelograms identified using the Distance Formula.

worked_examples.final_answer:
1) Isosceles (BC = AC = 2√13, AB = 8)

2) Yes, it is a parallelogram (both pairs of opposite sides equal: WX=YZ=5, XY=WZ=5)

3) Isosceles: EF = DF = 2√5, DE = 4
worked_examples.concept_applied: Distance Formula used to calculate all side lengths. Triangle classification: isosceles = two sides equal. Parallelogram: both pairs of opposite sides equal.

sections.introduction: The Distance Formula is the algebraic foundation of the equation of a circle. A circle is defined as the set of all points equidistant from a center point. Translating this definition into coordinates using the Distance Formula produces the standard equation of a circle.

introduction_image.alt: A circle centered at (h, k) with radius r. A point (x, y) on the circle is connected to the center by a radius. The Distance Formula applied: √((x−h)² + (y−k)²) = r, which squares to (x−h)² + (y−k)² = r² — the equation of the circle.
introduction_image.caption: The Distance Formula is the algebraic expression of 'all points r units from the center.'

key_concept.definition: A circle with center (h, k) and radius r consists of all points (x, y) whose distance from (h, k) is exactly r. Using the Distance Formula: $\sqrt{(x-h)^2 + (y-k)^2} = r$. Squaring both sides gives the standard equation of a circle: $(x-h)^2 + (y-k)^2 = r^2$.
key_concept.context: The Distance Formula provides the transition from geometric circles to algebraic equations. Given the equation of a circle, you can identify its center and radius. Given the center and radius, you can write the equation. This is a direct application of the Distance Formula.

image.alt: A circle with center (3, 4) and radius 5. Several points on the circle are shown with their distance to (3,4) labeled as 5. The equation (x−3)² + (y−4)² = 25 is displayed. Arrows show that every point (x,y) on the circle satisfies the Distance Formula equation.
image.caption: Circle equation: (x−3)² + (y−4)² = 25 means every point on the circle is 5 units from (3,4).

classifications.type: Writing Circle Equations from Center and Radius
classifications.value: Given the center (h, k) and radius r, substitute into $(x-h)^2 + (y-k)^2 = r^2$.
characteristics: Center (h, k) appears as subtraction in the formula: (x − h) and (y − k)
Radius r is squared on the right side: r²
If the center is at the origin (0, 0), the equation simplifies to $x^2 + y^2 = r^2$
classifications.behavior: The circle equation is the Distance Formula applied to all points of the circle. Writing it is straightforward substitution.

examples.process: Writing the equation of a circle
examples.explanation:
Center (2, −3), radius 4.

Equation: $(x-2)^2 + (y-(-3))^2 = 4^2$

$(x-2)^2 + (y+3)^2 = 16$

Any point (x, y) satisfying this is exactly 4 units from (2, −3).

image.alt: Circle centered at (2, −3) with radius 4. The equation (x−2)² + (y+3)² = 16 displayed. A point (6, −3) on the circle is highlighted, showing distance = √((6−2)² + (−3+3)²) = √16 = 4.
image.caption: Center (2, −3), r = 4 → (x−2)² + (y+3)² = 16.

classifications.type: Finding Center and Radius from an Equation
classifications.value: Given the equation $(x-h)^2 + (y-k)^2 = r^2$, the center is (h, k) and the radius is r.
characteristics: Center: (h, k) — note the signs: (x − h) means center x = h; (x + h) means center x = −h
Radius: the square root of the constant term on the right side
If the equation is not in standard form, complete the square for both x and y terms
classifications.behavior: Reading a circle equation reverses the Distance Formula. The equation encodes the set of all points at distance r from (h, k).

examples.explanation:
Equation: $(x-5)^2 + (y+2)^2 = 49$

Center: (5, −2) — note y+2 means y−(−2), so k = −2.

Radius: $\sqrt{49} = 7$.

image.alt: Circle equation (x−5)² + (y+2)² = 49. Annotations: (x−5) → h=5, (y+2) = (y−(−2)) → k=−2. r²=49 → r=7. The circle is drawn with center (5,−2) and radius 7.
image.caption: From equation to geometry: center (5, −2), radius 7.

image.alt: A comprehensive diagram connecting the Distance Formula to the circle equation. Left: Distance Formula. Center: the definition of a circle (all points r from center). Right: the standard equation (x−h)² + (y−k)² = r². Arrows show the logical flow from formula to equation.
image.caption: The Distance Formula is the algebraic bridge from the geometric definition of a circle to its equation.

sections.section_title: Common Mistakes with the Distance Formula
sections.introduction: The Distance Formula involves multiple steps—subtraction, squaring, adding, and square roots. Errors can occur at any stage. Recognizing common mistakes helps avoid them on homework and tests.

key_concept.definition: Common errors with the Distance Formula include: forgetting to square the differences, adding before squaring, mishandling negative signs, forgetting the square root, and confusing the Distance Formula with the Midpoint Formula or Slope Formula.
key_concept.context: These errors often come from rushing through the formula or from not understanding why each step exists. Remembering that this is the Pythagorean Theorem in coordinates helps make sense of the process.

image.alt: Four warning panels: (1) 'Square FIRST, then add' — not add then square. (2) 'Negative signs disappear when squared' — (−3)² = +9. (3) 'Take the square root last' — don't forget it. (4) 'Distance is a number, not a point' — don't confuse with Midpoint Formula.
image.caption: Common Distance Formula errors: order of operations, sign handling, missing square root, and formula confusion.

classifications.value: Writing $d = \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1 + y_2 - y_1)^2}$ or adding the differences without squaring first.
characteristics: Error: $d = \sqrt{(\Delta x + \Delta y)^2}$ — adding before squaring gives the wrong result
Correct: Square EACH difference separately, then add the squares
The Pythagorean Theorem is a² + b² = c², NOT (a + b)² = c²
classifications.behavior: The legs of the right triangle are separate. Their squares must be calculated independently and then added. (a + b)² = a² + 2ab + b², which is NOT the same as a² + b².

examples.explanation:
A(1, 2) and B(5, 6). Δx = 4, Δy = 4.

Incorrect: $\sqrt{(4 + 4)^2} = \sqrt{64} = 8$ (wrong).

Correct: $\sqrt{4^2 + 4^2} = \sqrt{16+16} = \sqrt{32} = 4\sqrt{2}$.

The incorrect method gives 8 instead of 4√2 ≈ 5.66.

image.alt: Two calculations compared: (1) Incorrect: √((4+4)²) = √64 = 8 with red X. (2) Correct: √(4²+4²) = √(16+16) = √32 = 4√2 with green check. Right triangle shown with legs 4 and 4, hypotenuse 4√2.
image.caption: Square each leg first, then add. (a+b)² ≠ a² + b².

classifications.note: Solution: Always follow the order: subtract → square each difference → add the squares → take the square root. Think of the Pythagorean Theorem: the squares are added, never the lengths.

classifications.value: Forgetting that a negative number squared becomes positive, or making sign errors in subtraction.
characteristics: Error: (x₂ − x₁)² is sometimes incorrectly left negative, or the negative sign is dropped before squaring
Correct: Any real number squared is positive. (−3)² = +9, not −9
Subtraction order does not matter because of the square: (1−4)² = (−3)² = 9 = (4−1)²
classifications.behavior: The square eliminates all sign issues. You can always subtract in whichever order gives positive differences for easier mental math. A negative squared is positive—this is a common point of arithmetic error.

examples.explanation:
A(−2, 5) and B(3, 1).

Δx = 3 − (−2) = 5, but if you do −2 − 3 = −5, the square is still 25.

Δy = 1 − 5 = −4 → (−4)² = 16, not −16.

$d = \sqrt{25 + 16} = \sqrt{41}$.

image.alt: Points A(−2,5) and B(3,1). Calculation shown: Δx = 3−(−2)=5, Δy = 1−5=−4. Both squared: 25 and 16. Warning: '(−4)² = +16, not −16. Negatives disappear when squared.'
image.caption: Negative differences become positive when squared. (−4)² = +16.

classifications.note: Solution: After squaring, all values are positive. If you get a negative number under the square root, you made a sign error. Remember: a square is always non-negative.

classifications.value: Leaving the answer as d² instead of taking the square root to find d.
characteristics: Error: Reporting $d = (x_2-x_1)^2 + (y_2-y_1)^2$ (the squared distance)
Correct: $d = \sqrt{(x_2-x_1)^2 + (y_2-y_1)^2}$ — the square root of that sum
The Distance Formula gives d, not d²
classifications.behavior: The square root is the final step of the Pythagorean Theorem: c² = a² + b², so c = √(a² + b²). Leaving the answer as a² + b² gives the square of the distance, not the distance itself.

examples.explanation:
A(0, 0) and B(3, 4). Δx = 3, Δy = 4.

Squares: 9 + 16 = 25 → this is d², not d.

Distance: d = √25 = 5.

Reporting 25 as the distance is off by a factor of 5.

image.alt: Segment from (0,0) to (3,4). The squared distance d² = 25 is shown, then the square root gives d = 5. Red X over 'd = 25' and green check over 'd = 5'.
image.caption: The Distance Formula includes the square root. 5 is the distance; 25 is d².

classifications.note: Solution: The Distance Formula starts with a square root symbol. Don't forget it. The answer should be d = √(sum), not d² = sum.

classifications.type: Confusing Distance with Midpoint or Slope
classifications.value: Using the wrong formula for the question asked.
characteristics: Distance = length (a number): $d = \sqrt{(x_2-x_1)^2 + (y_2-y_1)^2}$
Midpoint = center point (ordered pair): $M\left(\frac{x_1+x_2}{2}, \frac{y_1+y_2}{2}\right)$
Slope = steepness (a number): $m = \frac{y_2-y_1}{x_2-x_1}$
Each answers a different question about the segment
classifications.behavior: Before applying any formula, identify what the question asks: 'How long?' → Distance. 'Where is the center?' → Midpoint. 'How steep?' → Slope. Using the wrong formula produces a meaningless answer.

image.alt: Segment from (2,3) to (8,11). Three answers shown: Distance = 10 (a number), Midpoint = (5,7) (a point), Slope = 4/3 (a ratio). Each labeled with what it measures.
image.caption: Distance, Midpoint, and Slope are three different formulas for three different properties.

classifications.note: Solution: Before calculating, state in words what you are finding. 'I am finding the LENGTH, so I use the Distance Formula.' This prevents formula mix-ups.

worked_examples.title: Correcting Distance Formula Errors
worked_examples.problem:
A student finds the distance between A(2, 5) and B(6, 8) and gets each of the following wrong answers. Identify the error in each.

1) d = 7

2) d = (4, 3)

3) d = 25

4) d = 1

step_by_step.explanation:
The student likely added before squaring: √((4+3)²) = √49 = 7.

Correct: Δx = 4, Δy = 3. d = √(4² + 3²) = √(16+9) = √25 = 5.

Error: adding the legs (4+3=7) instead of using the Pythagorean Theorem.

The student used the Midpoint Formula or just wrote the coordinate differences as a point.

Correct: distance is a single number, not an ordered pair. The answer should be d = 5, not (4, 3).

Error: confusing the Distance Formula (gives a number) with the Midpoint Formula (gives a point) or simply listing the differences.

The student calculated d² correctly (16+9=25) but forgot to take the square root.

Correct: d = √25 = 5.

Error: stopped at d² instead of taking the square root.

The student may have used the Slope Formula: (8−5)/(6−2) = 3/4 = 0.75, not 1. Or they may have subtracted and divided incorrectly.

Correct: d = 5. The answer 1 has no clear relationship to the problem.

Error: unclear—possibly confusing the distance with some other calculation.

image.alt: Four panels: (1) d=7 crossed out, correct d=5 with 'added legs, didn't use Pythagorean Theorem'. (2) d=(4,3) crossed out, correct d=5 with 'used Midpoint Formula or listed differences'. (3) d=25 crossed out, correct d=5 with 'forgot square root'. (4) d=1 crossed out with 'formula confusion'.
image.caption: Common errors analyzed: adding legs, confusing formulas, forgetting square root.

worked_examples.final_answer:
The correct distance between A(2, 5) and B(6, 8) is d = 5.

Error 1: Added legs instead of using Pythagorean Theorem.

Error 2: Gave an ordered pair — distance is a single number.

Error 3: Gave d² instead of d (forgot square root).

Error 4: Unknown formula error.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Distance = √(Δx² + Δy²). Follow the order: square each difference, add, then square root. The answer is a single positive number.

sections.section_title: Real-World Applications of the Distance Formula
sections.introduction: The Distance Formula has countless practical applications—from calculating direct distances between locations on maps to determining the range of wireless signals. Any situation where positions are given as coordinates can use the Distance Formula to find how far apart they are.

categories.description: GPS systems use the Distance Formula (in three dimensions, with latitude, longitude, and altitude) to calculate straight-line distances between any two points on Earth.

examples.explanation: On a simplified coordinate map, City A is at (15, 30) and City B is at (55, 60), where units are in miles. The straight-line ('as the crow flies') distance is $\sqrt{(55-15)^2 + (60-30)^2} = \sqrt{1600 + 900} = \sqrt{2500} = 50$ miles.

image.alt: A simplified map grid with City A at (15, 30) and City B at (55, 60). A straight dashed line connects them labeled '50 miles (straight-line distance)'. A curved road route is also shown labeled '65 miles (driving distance)'. The right triangle legs are 40 and 30 miles.
image.caption: The Distance Formula gives the direct straight-line distance between locations.

examples.explanation: A cell tower at coordinates (0, 0) has a range of 10 miles. Will a phone at (8, 6) receive signal? Distance = $\sqrt{8^2 + 6^2} = \sqrt{100} = 10$ miles. The phone is exactly at the edge of the range.

image.alt: A cell tower at the origin (0,0) with a circle of radius 10 miles. A phone at (8,6) is shown on the circle. Distance calculated: √(64+36) = 10 miles. Label: 'Phone is exactly at the maximum range.'
image.caption: The Distance Formula determines if a device is within range of a signal tower.

categories.description: Builders and surveyors use the Distance Formula to calculate distances between points on a site plan, verify right angles, and ensure structures are built to specification.

examples.explanation: A rectangular foundation has corners at (0,0), (12,0), (12,8), and (0,8). To verify it is truly rectangular (not a parallelogram), check that diagonals are equal: diagonal from (0,0) to (12,8) = $\sqrt{144+64} = \sqrt{208}$. Diagonal from (12,0) to (0,8) = $\sqrt{144+64} = \sqrt{208}$. Equal diagonals confirm a rectangle.

image.alt: A rectangular foundation plan with corners at (0,0), (12,0), (12,8), (0,8). Both diagonals drawn and calculated: each is √208. Equal diagonals confirm the foundation is a true rectangle, not a skewed parallelogram.
image.caption: Equal diagonals (calculated with the Distance Formula) verify a rectangular foundation.

examples.explanation: A diagonal brace is needed from corner (0, 0) to corner (10, 6) of a frame, where units are in feet. The brace length = $\sqrt{10^2 + 6^2} = \sqrt{100 + 36} = \sqrt{136} = 2\sqrt{34} \approx 11.66$ feet. This exact calculation prevents material waste.

image.alt: A rectangular frame with a diagonal brace from (0,0) to (10,6). The brace length calculated as √136 ≈ 11.66 feet. A tape measure icon next to the brace with the exact measurement.
image.caption: The Distance Formula gives the exact length of a diagonal brace for construction.

categories.description: The Distance Formula calculates shot distances, determines if a player is within a certain range, and powers the physics engines of video games.

examples.explanation: A soccer player at field position (20, 15) shoots at a goal centered at (80, 25), where units are in yards. The shot distance = $\sqrt{(80-20)^2 + (25-15)^2} = \sqrt{3600 + 100} = \sqrt{3700} \approx 60.8$ yards.

image.alt: A soccer field diagram with a player at (20, 15) and the goal at (80, 25). A dotted line shows the shot path. Distance calculated as √3700 ≈ 60.8 yards. The right triangle legs (60, 10) are shown.
image.caption: Shot distance calculated with the Distance Formula: ≈ 60.8 yards.

examples.explanation: In a game, a player character at (150, 200) and an enemy at (210, 280) are on screen (pixel coordinates). The game engine calculates the distance between them: $\sqrt{(210-150)^2 + (280-200)^2} = \sqrt{3600 + 6400} = \sqrt{10000} = 100$ pixels. If 100 < attack range, the enemy is in range.

image.alt: A game screen with player at (150, 200) and enemy at (210, 280). Distance = 100 pixels calculated. A circle of attack range (120 pixels) around the player encloses the enemy. Label: 'Enemy in range: 100 < 120.'
image.caption: Video game engines use the Distance Formula for collision detection and range checks.

sections.introduction: Building fluency with the Distance Formula requires practice with integer coordinates, negative values, radical simplification, and applications to polygon classification. Work through these problems to develop speed and accuracy.

worked_examples.title: Comprehensive Distance Formula Practice
worked_examples.problem:
1) Find the distance between A(3, 1) and B(15, 6).

2) Find the distance between C(−4, 2) and D(8, −3).

3) Find the exact distance between E(1, 5) and F(7, 11) in simplified radical form.

4) Classify triangle PQR with P(0, 0), Q(12, 0), R(6, 8) by side lengths.

5) Determine if quadrilateral JKLM with J(0,0), K(6,0), L(8,3), M(2,3) is a parallelogram.

6) A circle has center (4, −1) and radius 6. Write its equation.

step_by_step.explanation:
$d = \sqrt{(8-(-4))^2 + (-3-2)^2} = \sqrt{12^2 + (-5)^2}$

$= \sqrt{144 + 25} = \sqrt{169} = 13$

Also a 5-12-13 triple! The negative signs disappear when squared.

PQ = $\sqrt{12^2 + 0^2} = 12$

QR = $\sqrt{(6-12)^2 + (8-0)^2} = \sqrt{36+64} = \sqrt{100} = 10$

PR = $\sqrt{6^2 + 8^2} = \sqrt{36+64} = \sqrt{100} = 10$

QR = PR = 10, PQ = 12. Two sides equal → isosceles triangle.

JK = $\sqrt{(6-0)^2 + 0^2} = 6$

LM = $\sqrt{(8-2)^2 + (3-3)^2} = 6$ → JK = LM ✓

KL = $\sqrt{(8-6)^2 + (3-0)^2} = \sqrt{4+9} = \sqrt{13}$

JM = $\sqrt{(2-0)^2 + (3-0)^2} = \sqrt{4+9} = \sqrt{13}$ → KL = JM ✓

Both pairs of opposite sides equal → parallelogram.

image.alt: Six panels: (1) Segment from (3,1) to (15,6), d=13. (2) Segment from (−4,2) to (8,−3), d=13. (3) Segment from (1,5) to (7,11), d=6√2. (4) Isosceles triangle with sides 12, 10, 10. (5) Parallelogram with sides 6, √13, 6, √13. (6) Circle center (4,−1), r=6.
image.caption: Comprehensive Distance Formula practice: distances, polygon classification, and circle equations.

worked_examples.final_answer:
1) d = 13

2) d = 13

3) d = 6√2

4) Isosceles triangle (QR = PR = 10, PQ = 12)

5) Yes, JKLM is a parallelogram

6) (x−4)² + (y+1)² = 36
worked_examples.concept_applied: Distance Formula: √(Δx² + Δy²). Pythagorean triples, radical simplification, polygon classification by side length, and the equation of a circle.

summary: The Distance Formula calculates the length of a segment in the coordinate plane. Key points: 1) Formula: $d = \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2}$. It is the Pythagorean Theorem expressed in coordinates: the segment is the hypotenuse of a right triangle with legs $\Delta x$ and $\Delta y$. 2) Process: Subtract coordinates to find changes, square each change, add the squares, take the square root. The order of subtraction does not matter because the differences are squared. 3) Special cases: Horizontal segments (y₁ = y₂) simplify to $d = |x_2 - x_1|$. Vertical segments (x₁ = x₂) simplify to $d = |y_2 - y_1|$. Pythagorean triples produce integer distances. Non-perfect squares stay in simplified radical form. 4) Polygon Classification: The Distance Formula classifies triangles (equilateral, isosceles, scalene) and quadrilaterals (square, rectangle, rhombus, parallelogram) by calculating side lengths and comparing them. 5) Circle Equation: A circle is all points at distance r from center (h, k). Applying the Distance Formula gives $(x-h)^2 + (y-k)^2 = r^2$. 6) Common errors: Adding before squaring, mishandling negative signs (a negative squared is positive), forgetting the final square root, and confusing the Distance Formula (gives a number) with Midpoint (gives a point) or Slope (gives a ratio). 7) Applications: Navigation distances, GPS technology, construction verification, sports analytics, and video game collision detection. The Distance Formula is essential for coordinate proofs and all advanced coordinate geometry.

review_questions_image.alt: Concept map with 'Distance Formula' in center. Branches: Derivation (Pythagorean Theorem, right triangle with legs Δx and Δy), Formula (d = √((x₂−x₁)² + (y₂−y₁)²)), Special Cases (horizontal, vertical, Pythagorean triples), Applications (polygon classification, circle equation, real-world distances), Common Errors (add before square, sign errors, missing square root, formula confusion).
review_questions_image.caption: Distance Formula Review: A visual concept map connecting the Pythagorean Theorem, the formula, special cases, applications, and error prevention.
